codeSnapshot.label: bahdanau_attention.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class BahdanauAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Additive (Bahdanau) Attention — aligns decoder state with encoder outputs.
    score(s_{i-1}, h_j) = v_a^T tanh(W_a s_{i-1} + U_a h_j)
    """
    def __init__(self, hidden_dim: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.W_a = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim, bias=False)  # decoder state
        self.U_a = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim, bias=False)  # encoder outputs
        self.v_a = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1, bias=False)           # score projection

    def forward(self, decoder_state, encoder_outputs):
        """
        decoder_state:   [B, 1, hidden]  (current decoder hidden state)
        encoder_outputs: [B, T_src, hidden]  (all encoder hidden states)
        returns:
            context:  [B, hidden]          (weighted sum of encoder outputs)
            weights:  [B, T_src]           (attention distribution)
        """
        # Broadcast decoder state to [B, T_src, hidden]
        dec = self.W_a(decoder_state)         # [B, 1, hidden]
        enc = self.U_a(encoder_outputs)       # [B, T_src, hidden]
        # Compute alignment scores
        energy = self.v_a(torch.tanh(dec + enc)).squeeze(-1)  # [B, T_src]
        weights = F.softmax(energy, dim=-1)                   # [B, T_src]
        # Compute context vector as weighted sum
        context = torch.bmm(weights.unsqueeze(1), encoder_outputs).squeeze(1)  # [B, hidden]
        return context, weights

# Usage
B, T_src, hidden = 4, 20, 256
encoder_outputs = torch.randn(B, T_src, hidden)
decoder_state   = torch.randn(B, 1, hidden)

attn = BahdanauAttention(hidden)
context, weights = attn(decoder_state, encoder_outputs)
print(f"Context:  {context.shape}")   # [4, 256]
print(f"Weights:  {weights.shape}")   # [4, 20] (sum to 1)
print(f"Weight sum: {weights.sum(dim=-1)}")  # all ~1.0

eli3: The basic seq2seq model reads an entire sentence into one vector before translating — like reading a whole paragraph, closing your eyes, and then trying to write it in another language from memory. For long sentences, you forget the beginning by the time you reach the end.

Attention lets the decoder 'look back' at each word in the input while generating each word in the output. When generating the French word for 'bank' in a translation, the model can focus on the English word 'bank' in the original sentence — and the French word for 'river' if that's the context. The model learns where to look.

This is the pre-Transformer attention mechanism (Bahdanau, 2015). The model computes a score for how relevant each input word is to the current output step, turns those scores into probabilities (attention weights), and uses a weighted average of input representations as the context. The attention weights can even be visualised as a translation alignment matrix.

whenWhy.title: Long-sequence translation and summarisation
whenWhy.body: Bahdanau attention was specifically designed to fix the degradation of seq2seq performance on long sequences. Without attention, BLEU scores drop sharply for source sentences with 30+ tokens. With attention, performance degrades much more gracefully because the decoder has direct access to all encoder states at each step — it no longer needs to compress everything into a single context vector. This is fundamental for document summarisation, where the source may be hundreds of tokens long.

whenWhy.title: Alignment and interpretability
whenWhy.body: Attention weights produce an alignment matrix: a T_src × T_tgt matrix where each cell α_{ij} indicates how much attention the decoder paid to encoder position j when generating output position i. For translation, this matrix is interpretable as a word alignment and can be visualised as a heatmap. Systems that use attention can explain which input tokens drove each output token — valuable in regulated domains (medical, legal translation) where auditors need to understand model reasoning.

whenWhy.title: Precursor to self-attention and Transformers
whenWhy.body: Bahdanau attention is the direct ancestor of the scaled dot-product attention in Transformers. The core operation — compute alignment scores, apply softmax, compute weighted sum of value vectors — is identical. Transformers generalised this by: (1) allowing queries, keys, and values to come from the same sequence (self-attention); (2) using dot product instead of additive scoring (faster); (3) scaling by √d_k (to stabilise softmax); (4) applying multiple heads in parallel. Understanding Bahdanau attention makes Transformer attention much clearer.

deepDiveIntro: Bahdanau et al. (2015) introduced the additive attention mechanism for neural machine translation. The encoder produces a sequence of annotations h_j (encoder hidden states at each position j). At decoder step i, the context vector c_i is computed as a weighted sum of all encoder annotations: c_i = sum_j alpha_{ij} h_j. The alignment weights alpha_{ij} = softmax(e_{ij}) are normalised scores: e_{ij} = a(s_{i-1}, h_j), where a is a small feedforward network (alignment model): a(s, h) = v_a^T tanh(W_a s + U_a h). Here s_{i-1} is the decoder's previous hidden state, and v_a, W_a, U_a are learned parameters of the alignment model. The attention mechanism adds a relatively small number of parameters (W_a, U_a ∈ ℝ^{n×n}, v_a ∈ ℝ^n) but dramatically improves long-sequence performance. [1]

Luong et al. (2015) introduced two alternative scoring functions: dot-product attention score(s, h) = s^T h (no parameters, requires same dimension for s and h); general attention score(s, h) = s^T W_a h (W_a ∈ ℝ^{n×n} — one matrix). These multiplicative (Luong) attention variants are computationally cheaper than additive (Bahdanau) attention and achieved comparable or better performance on translation tasks. Dot-product attention is the basis for Transformer scaled dot-product attention (divided by √d_k to prevent small gradients at large dimensions): Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k) V. The evolution from Bahdanau's additive scoring → Luong's dot-product → Vaswani's scaled dot-product is a direct lineage. [2]

deepDive.heading: Alignment Score Functions
deepDive.body: Computationally heavier (requires tanh and projection), but works for s and h with different dimensions. No constraint on the relationship between s and h. Requires equal dimensions. Computationally cheap but can produce very large/small values for large dimensions. Requires learnable W_a but no dimension constraint.

deepDive.heading: Context Vector and Decoder Integration
deepDive.body: The context vector c_i is fed to the decoder in two ways: (1) Concat approach (Bahdanau): concatenate c_i with the decoder input before the RNN step — c_i is part of the input at each step. (2) Input-feeding approach (Luong): concatenate c_i with the decoder output h_i after the RNN step, then project to vocabulary. The input-feeding approach allows the decoder to be aware of previous alignment decisions when making new ones (c_i depends on s_{i-1} which saw c_{i-1}).

deepDive.heading: Attention Weight Visualisation and Monotonic Attention
deepDive.body: For translation between related languages (English-French), the matrix is approximately diagonal — source and target words align in roughly the same order. For distant language pairs (English-Japanese), the matrix shows long-range non-monotonic alignments (e.g., SOV word order in Japanese vs SVO in English). Monotonic attention (Raffel et al., 2017) enforces strictly left-to-right alignment for tasks where the output is naturally aligned with the input in order (speech recognition, text normalisation). Monotonic attention reduces this to O(1) amortised, enabling streaming inference.

deepDive.heading: From Bahdanau Attention to Self-Attention
deepDive.body: In cross-attention (encoder-decoder attention), queries come from the decoder and keys/values come from the encoder. Self-attention is the case where queries, keys, and values all come from the same sequence — each position attends to all other positions in the same sequence. Self-attention was implicit in the memory-augmented neural network literature (Neural Turing Machines, 2014) and was formalised in the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017). The key insight: if the encoder-decoder attention allows the decoder to focus on relevant input positions, self-attention allows each position in any sequence to focus on relevant positions within the same sequence — enabling global dependency modelling without recurrence. This replaces the O(T) path length of RNNs with O(1) direct connections between any pair of positions. [2]

takeaways: Bahdanau attention: c_i = Σ_j α_{ij}h_j where α_{ij} = softmax(v_a^T tanh(W_a s_{i-1} + U_a h_j)). [1]
Attention solves the fixed-context-vector bottleneck: decoder attends to all encoder states, performance holds for long inputs. [1]
Alignment weights α are interpretable as a translation alignment matrix — visualisable as a heatmap. [1]
Luong scaled dot-product: score = s^T h/√d_k — computationally cheaper; basis for Transformer self-attention. [2]
Self-attention generalises cross-attention by attending within the same sequence — replaces RNN sequential processing. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why is the attention weight vector scaled by √d_k in scaled dot-product attention?
edgeCases.a: For query q ∈ ℝ^d and key k ∈ ℝ^d with components drawn from N(0,1), the dot product q·k = Σ_i q_i k_i has variance d (sum of d products, each with variance 1). The standard deviation is √d, so dot products have magnitude O(√d). For large d (e.g., d=512, √d≈22), softmax receives inputs with magnitude ~22, pushing it into saturation regions where the gradient is near zero — the softmax output is concentrated near one-hot (e.g., [0.99, 0.005, 0.005,...]) with gradients ≈0. Dividing by √d keeps the dot products at unit variance, preserving non-saturated softmax gradients regardless of dimension. This is why scaled dot-product (÷√d_k) works better than plain dot-product for high-dimensional keys.

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between cross-attention and self-attention?
edgeCases.a: In cross-attention (used in seq2seq decoder): queries Q come from the decoder (current sequence being generated), keys K and values V come from the encoder (source sequence). Each decoder position asks 'which source positions are relevant to me?'. In self-attention (used in both encoder and decoder): Q, K, V all come from the same sequence. Each position in the sequence asks 'which other positions in my own sequence are relevant to me?'. Self-attention enables modelling of intra-sequence dependencies — e.g., resolving pronoun coreference, understanding that 'bank' refers to a river or a financial institution based on other words in the same sentence. Transformer encoders use only self-attention; decoders use both masked self-attention (attend only to past positions) and cross-attention (attend to encoder outputs).

sources.label: Bahdanau, Cho & Bengio (2015) — Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate, ICLR 2015 arXiv:1409.0473
Luong, Pham & Manning (2015) — Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation, EMNLP 2015 arXiv:1508.04025
